title: Will falling populations reshape energy demand? | Wood Mackenzie
gist: Simon Flowers argues plummeting global fertility rates could push population far below UN projections, potentially peaking at 8.9 billion in 2053 under the low scenario, which would reshape long-term energy and commodity demand while reinforcing the AI-automation-driven bias toward electricity and critical minerals over hydrocarbons.

themes.theme: Demographic decline as a planning risk

insights.claim: The intensifying downward trend in fertility rates is now a major new risk factor for energy strategic planners refreshing long-term outlooks, alongside the war in Iran.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Demographics is entering core energy demand modeling, potentially lowering long-run demand baselines.
insights.quote: No less important is how the intensifying downward trend in fertility rates might affect global populations and energy demand in the longer term.
insights.locator: The impact of the war in Iran on energy markets

insights.claim: The global fertility rate fell from 2.6 births per woman in 2007 to 2.2 in 2025, perilously close to the 2.1 replacement ratio needed to keep the population stable.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Approaching sub-replacement fertility signals structural demand headwinds beyond current forecast horizons.
insights.quote: The global fertility rate fell from 2.6 births per woman in 2007 to 2.2 in 2025, perilously close to the 2.1 replacement ratio
insights.locator: The decline in fertility rates will simultaneously

insights.claim: China's birth rate fell to 5.6 births per 1,000 people in 2025, the lowest ever recorded, and its population contracted by 3.4 million to 1.40 billion, 9.6 million below the UN's 2024 projection.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: China already undershooting UN forecasts is the clearest signal that global demand models are too optimistic.
insights.quote: China's population contracted by 3.4 million people last year and now stands at 1.40 billion – 9.6 million below the UN projection from 2024
insights.locator: China's birth rate, for example, fell to 5.6 births

insights.claim: Under the UN's low birth rate scenario, global population could peak at 8.9 billion in 2053 and fall to 7.0 billion by 2100, back to 2011 levels, with a new UN World Population Prospects due July 2026 likely to reduce projections.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: A downgraded UN forecast in July 2026 is a near-term catalyst that could trigger industry-wide demand model revisions.
insights.quote: the global population could decline even within our forecast horizon to 2060, peaking at 8.9 billion in 2053
insights.locator: Observed reductions in fertility rates suggest

themes.theme: Why energy demand stays robust anyway

insights.claim: Most drivers of energy demand remain robust through 2060 regardless of demographic downgrade, since even a pessimistic scenario adds around 700 million people by 2060 and vast unmet energy needs persist across Asia and Africa.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Near-to-mid-term energy demand thesis is intact; the demographic risk is a post-2060 structural story.
insights.quote: even a pessimistic scenario would see an increase of around 700 million people by 2060 from current levels
insights.locator: First, most of the drivers of energy demand appear to be robust

insights.claim: Wood Mackenzie forecasts global primary energy consumption rising 8% to peak at 717 exajoules in 2035 before declining to 672 EJ by 2060, while electricity consumption doubles to 71 petawatt-hours over the period.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Electricity doubling while primary energy peaks and declines quantifies the electrification/decoupling trade.
insights.quote: reaching a peak of 717 exajoules (EJ)* in 2035, before gradually declining to 672 EJ by 2060. Electricity consumption continues to grow though the period, doubling to 71 petawatt-hours
insights.locator: We currently forecast global primary energy consumption

themes.theme: AI automation and the electrons-over-molecules bias

insights.claim: Lower population would exacerbate the incentive to invest heavily in AI-driven automation across all sectors as workforces shrink, embedding upside for electricity and critical minerals and downside for hydrocarbon molecules.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Demographic decline structurally favors electrification and critical-minerals demand over oil and gas.
insights.quote: Shrinking workforces will increase the incentive to invest heavily in AI-driven automation across all sectors, embedding the upside for electrons and related critical minerals – and a downside for molecules.
insights.locator: Second, the impact of lower population

insights.claim: Automation could concentrate wealth among capital owners at the expense of labour, and fewer workers with reduced purchasing power will eventually limit the upside in consumer demand.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: The second-order risk is that inequality and weaker consumer demand cap the automation-led growth story.
insights.quote: automation could lead to social inequality – wealth concentrated among capital owners at the expense of labour

insights.claim: Governments must act decisively to secure capital for smart, clean grids before the demographic profile shifts dramatically after 2060, since AI-driven productivity gains could offset demographic drags if investment is made while finance is available.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: A closing investment window frames grid and clean-energy capex as time-sensitive; delay compounds demographic drag.
insights.quote: Governments, therefore, need to act decisively to secure private and public capital to ensure the energy system adapts and evolves before the demographic profile changes dramatically after 2060.

insights.claim: An earlier and lower global population peak is now viable and capable of making or breaking the next commodity super-cycle, depending on how the world manages demographics, economic growth, and technology.
insights.speaker: Simon Flowers
insights.so_what: Demographics is a swing variable for commodity super-cycle positioning; it could be headwind or catalyst.
insights.quote: an earlier and lower global population peak is now viable and capable of making or breaking the next commodity super-cycle
insights.locator: Finally, an earlier and lower global population peak
